examples.title: Test-and-Set Lock Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of mutual exclusion using atomic Test-and-Set instruction in C
examples.explanation: Test-and-Set atomically reads the old value of lock and sets it to true. The while loop continues (busy waits) if lock was already true (another process holds it). When it returns false, the current process has acquired the lock and can enter the critical section. The exit section simply sets lock to false.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for lock acquisition in best case, O(n) worst case where n is waiting processes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only uses one boolean variable

examples.title: Peterson's Algorithm Implementation
examples.description: Software solution for two-process mutual exclusion using shared variables
examples.code:
# Shared variables for two processes
flag = [False, False]  # flag[i] = True means process i wants to enter
turn = 0               # whose turn it is (0 or 1)

def process_entry(process_id):
    other = 1 - process_id
    flag[process_id] = True    # I want to enter
    turn = other               # Give priority to other
    
    # Wait while other wants to enter AND it's other's turn
    while flag[other] and turn == other:
        pass  # Busy wait - remain in entry section
    
    # Critical section starts here
    # ... access shared resources ...

def process_exit(process_id):
    flag[process_id] = False   # I am done with critical section
    # Remainder section continues
examples.explanation: Each process first declares its intention by setting flag[process_id]=True. Then it gives priority to the other process by setting turn=other. The while loop blocks this process if the other process wants to enter AND it's the other's turn. This ensures mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting for two processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) in worst case for entry
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - uses constant memory for flag array and turn variable

examples.title: Swap-based Mutual Exclusion
examples.description: Implementation using atomic Swap instruction to implement mutex lock
examples.explanation: Each process sets its local key to true and atomically swaps it with the shared lock. If the lock was already held (true), after swap, key becomes true and the process waits. If the lock was free (false), after swap, lock becomes true (held) and key becomes false (free), so the process enters its critical section.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average, can vary based on contention

examples.title: Spinlock with Memory Barrier
examples.description: Java implementation of spinlock with volatile keyword and memory barriers
examples.explanation: This Java implementation uses AtomicBoolean with compareAndSet which provides atomicity. The lock() method spins until it successfully sets the lock from false to true. Using volatile ensures visibility across threads. The try-finally pattern ensures the lock is always released even if an exception occurs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) average case, worst case unbounded if lock is held
